title: IX: Goyles
html:
A party of four were assembled next morning at a very late breakfast, or very early lunch, in Lord Peter‚Äôs flat. Its most cheerful member, despite a throbbing shoulder and a splitting headache, was undoubtedly Lord Peter himself, who lay upon the Chesterfield surrounded with cushions and carousing upon tea and toast. Having been brought home in an ambulance, he had instantly fallen into a healing sleep, and had woken at nine o‚Äôclock aggressively clear and active in mind. In consequence, Mr.¬ÝParker had been dispatched in a hurry, half-fed and burdened with the secret memory of last night‚Äôs disclosures, to Scotland Yard. Here he had set in motion the proper machinery for catching Lord Peter‚Äôs assassin. ‚ÄúOnly don‚Äôt you say anything about the attack on me,‚Äù said his lordship. ‚ÄúTell ‚Äôem he‚Äôs to be detained in connection with the Riddlesdale case. That‚Äôs good enough for them.‚Äù It was now eleven, and Mr.¬ÝParker had returned, gloomy and hungry, and was consuming a belated omelette and a glass of claret.

Lady Mary Wimsey was hunched up in the window-seat. Her bobbed golden hair made a little blur of light about her in the pale autumn sunshine. She had made an attempt to breakfast earlier, and now sat gazing out into Piccadilly. Her first appearance that morning had been made in Lord Peter‚Äôs dressing-gown, but she now wore a serge skirt and jade-green jumper, which had been brought to town for her by the fourth member of the party, now composedly eating a mixed grill and sharing the decanter with Parker.

This was a rather short, rather plump, very brisk elderly lady, with bright black eyes like a bird‚Äôs, and very handsome white hair exquisitely dressed. Far from looking as though she had just taken a long night journey, she was easily the most composed and trim of the four. She was, however, annoyed, and said so at considerable length. This was the Dowager Duchess of Denver.

‚ÄúIt is not so much, Mary, that you went off so abruptly last nightÔªø‚Äîjust before dinner, tooÔªø‚Äîinconveniencing and alarming us very muchÔªø‚Äîindeed, poor Helen was totally unable to eat her dinner, which was extremely distressing to her feelings, because, you know, she always makes such a point of never being upset about anythingÔªø‚ÄîI really don‚Äôt know why, for some of the greatest men have not minded showing their feelings, I don‚Äôt mean Southerners necessarily, but, as Mr.¬ÝChesterton very rightly points outÔªø‚ÄîNelson, too, who was certainly English if he wasn‚Äôt Irish or Scotch, I forget, but United Kingdom, anyway (if that means anything nowadays with a Free StateÔªø‚Äîsuch a ridiculous title, especially as it always makes one think of the Orange Free State, and I‚Äôm sure they wouldn‚Äôt care to be mixed up with that, being so very green themselves). And going off without even proper clothes, and taking the car, so that I had to wait till the 1:15 from NorthallertonÔªø‚Äîa ridiculous time to start, and such a bad train, too, not getting up till 10:30. Besides, if you must run off to town, why do it in that unfinished manner? If you had only looked up the trains before starting you would have seen you would have half an hour‚Äôs wait at Northallerton, and you could quite easily have packed a bag. It‚Äôs so much better to do things neatly and thoroughlyÔªø‚Äîeven stupid things. And it was very stupid of you indeed to dash off like that, to embarrass and bore poor Mr.¬ÝParker with a lot of twaddleÔªø‚Äîthough I suppose it was Peter you meant to see. You know, Peter, if you will haunt low places full of Russians and sucking Socialists taking themselves seriously, you ought to know better than to encourage them by running after them, however futile, and given to drinking coffee and writing poems with no shape to them, and generally ruining their nerves. And, in any case, it makes not the slightest difference; I could have told Peter all about it myself, if he doesn‚Äôt know already, as he probably does.‚Äù

Lady Mary turned very white at this and glanced at Parker, who replied rather to her than to the Dowager:

‚ÄúNo, Lord Peter and I haven‚Äôt had time to discuss anything yet.‚Äù

‚ÄúLest it should ruin my shattered nerves and bring a fever to my aching brow,‚Äù added that nobleman amiably. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre a kind, thoughtful soul, Charles, and I don‚Äôt know what I should do without you. I wish that rotten old secondhand dealer had been a bit brisker about takin‚Äô in his stock-in-trade for the night, though. Perfectly ‚Äôstraor‚Äônary number of knobs there are on a brass bedstead. Saw it comin‚Äô, y‚Äôknow, an‚Äô couldn‚Äôt stop myself. However, what‚Äôs a mere brass bedstead? The great detective, though at first stunned and dizzy from his brutal treatment by the fifteen veiled assassins all armed with meat-choppers, soon regained his senses, thanks to his sound constitution and healthy manner of life. Despite the severe gassing he had endured in the underground roomÔªø‚Äîeh? A telegram? Oh, thanks, Bunter.‚Äù

Lord Peter appeared to read the message with great inward satisfaction, for his long lips twitched at the corners, and he tucked the slip of paper away in his pocketbook with a little sigh of satisfaction. He called to Bunter to take away the breakfast-tray and to renew the cooling bandage about his brow. This done, Lord Peter leaned back among his cushions, and with an air of malicious enjoyment launched at Mr.¬ÝParker the inquiry:

‚ÄúWell, now, how did you and Mary get on last night? Polly, did you tell him you‚Äôd done the murder?‚Äù

Few things are more irritating than to discover, after you have been at great pains to spare a person some painful intelligence, that he has known it all along and is not nearly so much affected by it as he properly should be. Mr.¬ÝParker quite simply and suddenly lost his temper. He bounded to his feet, and exclaimed, without the least reason: ‚ÄúOh, it‚Äôs perfectly hopeless trying to do anything!‚Äù

Lady Mary sprang from the window-seat.

‚ÄúYes, I did,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs quite true. Your precious case is finished, Peter.‚Äù

The Dowager said, without the least discomposure: ‚ÄúYou must allow your brother to be the best judge of his own affairs, my dear.‚Äù

‚ÄúAs a matter of fact,‚Äù replied his lordship, ‚ÄúI rather fancy Polly‚Äôs right. Hope so, I‚Äôm sure. Anyway, we‚Äôve got the fellow, so now we shall know.‚Äù

Lady Mary gave a sort of gasp, and stepped forward with her chin up and her hands tightly clenched. It caught at Parker‚Äôs heart to see overwhelming catastrophe so bravely faced. The official side of him was thoroughly bewildered, but the human part ranged itself instantly in support of that gallant defiance.

‚ÄúWhom have they got?‚Äù he demanded, in a voice quite unlike his own.

‚ÄúThe Goyles person,‚Äù said Lord Peter carelessly. ‚ÄúUncommon quick work, what? But since he‚Äôd no more original idea than to take the boat-train to Folkestone they didn‚Äôt have much difficulty.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt isn‚Äôt true,‚Äù said Lady Mary. She stamped. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a lie. He wasn‚Äôt there. He‚Äôs innocent. I killed Denis.‚Äù

‚ÄúFine,‚Äù thought Parker, ‚Äúfine! Damn Goyles, anyway, what‚Äôs he done to deserve it?‚Äù

Lord Peter said: ‚ÄúMary, don‚Äôt be an ass.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said the Dowager placidly. ‚ÄúI was going to suggest to you, Peter, that this Mr.¬ÝGoylesÔªø‚Äîsuch a terrible name, Mary dear, I can‚Äôt say I ever cared for it, even if there had been nothing else against himÔªø‚Äîespecially as he would sign himself Geo. GoylesÔªø‚ÄîG. e. o. you know, Mr.¬ÝParker, for George, and I never could help reading it as GargoylesÔªø‚ÄîI very nearly wrote to you, my dear, mentioning Mr.¬ÝGoyles, and asking if you could see him in town, because there was something, when I came to think of it, about that ipecacuanha business that made me feel he might have something to do with it.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Peter, with a grin, ‚Äúyou always did find him a bit sickenin‚Äô, didn‚Äôt you?‚Äù

‚ÄúHow can you, Wimsey?‚Äù growled Parker reproachfully, with his eyes on Mary‚Äôs face.

‚ÄúNever mind him,‚Äù said the girl. ‚ÄúIf you can‚Äôt be a gentleman, PeterÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúDamn it all!‚Äù cried the invalid explosively. ‚ÄúHere‚Äôs a fellow who, without the slightest provocation, plugs a bullet into my shoulder, breaks my collarbone, brings me up head foremost on a knobbly secondhand brass bedstead and vamooses, and when, in what seems to me jolly mild, parliamentary language, I call him a sickenin‚Äô feller my own sister says I‚Äôm no gentleman. Look at me! In my own house, forced to sit here with a perfectly beastly headache, and lap up toast and tea, while you people distend and bloat yourselves on mixed grills and omelettes and a damn good vintage claretÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúSilly boy,‚Äù said the Duchess, ‚Äúdon‚Äôt get so excited. And it‚Äôs time for your medicine. Mr.¬ÝParker, kindly touch the bell.‚Äù

Mr.¬ÝParker obeyed in silence. Lady Mary came slowly across, and stood looking at her brother.

‚ÄúPeter,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúwhat makes you say that he did it?‚Äù

‚ÄúDid what?‚Äù

‚ÄúShotÔªø‚Äîyou?‚Äù The words were only a whisper.

The entrance of Mr.¬ÝBunter at this moment with a cooling draught dissipated the tense atmosphere. Lord Peter quaffed his potion, had his pillows rearranged, submitted to have his temperature taken and his pulse counted, asked if he might not have an egg for his lunch, and lit a cigarette. Mr.¬ÝBunter retired, people distributed themselves into more comfortable chairs, and felt happier.

‚ÄúNow, Polly, old girl,‚Äù said Peter, ‚Äúcut out the sob-stuff. I accidentally ran into this Goyles chap last night at your Soviet Club. I asked that Miss Tarrant to introduce me, but the minute Goyles heard my name, he made tracks. I rushed out after him, only meanin‚Äô to have a word with him, when the idiot stopped at the corner of Newport Court, potted me, and bunked. Silly-ass thing to do. I knew who he was. He couldn‚Äôt help gettin‚Äô caught.‚Äù

‚ÄúPeterÔªø‚Äî‚Äù said Mary in a ghastly voice.

‚ÄúLook here, Polly,‚Äù said Wimsey. ‚ÄúI did think of you. Honest injun, I did. I haven‚Äôt had the man arrested. I‚Äôve made no charge at allÔªø‚Äîhave I, Parker? What did you tell ‚Äôem to do when you were down at the Yard this morning?‚Äù

‚ÄúTo detain Goyles pending inquiries, because he was wanted as a witness in the Riddlesdale case,‚Äù said Parker slowly.

‚ÄúHe knows nothing about it,‚Äù said Mary, doggedly now. ‚ÄúHe wasn‚Äôt anywhere near. He is innocent of that!‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you think so?‚Äù said Lord Peter gravely. ‚ÄúIf you know he is innocent, why tell all these lies to screen him? It won‚Äôt do, Mary. You know he was thereÔªø‚Äîand you think he is guilty.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo!‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Wimsey, grasping her with his sound hand as she shrank away. ‚ÄúMary, have you thought what you are doing? You are perjuring yourself and putting Gerald in peril of his life, in order to shield from justice a man whom you suspect of murdering your lover and who has most certainly tried to murder me.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh,‚Äù cried Parker, in an agony, ‚Äúall this interrogation is horribly irregular.‚Äù

‚ÄúNever mind him,‚Äù said Peter. ‚ÄúDo you really think you‚Äôre doing the right thing, Mary?‚Äù

The girl looked helplessly at her brother for a minute or two. Peter cocked up a whimsical, appealing eye from under his bandages. The defiance melted out of her face.

‚ÄúI‚Äôll tell the truth,‚Äù said Lady Mary.

‚ÄúGood egg,‚Äù said Peter, extending a hand. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry. I know you like the fellow, and we appreciate your decision enormously. Truly, we do. Now, sail ahead, old thing, and you take it down, Parker.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, it really all started years ago with George. You were at the Front then, Peter, but I suppose they told you about itÔªø‚Äîand put everything in the worst possible light.‚Äù

‚ÄúI wouldn‚Äôt say that, dear,‚Äù put in the Duchess. ‚ÄúI think I told Peter that your brother and I were not altogether pleased with what we had seen of the young manÔªø‚Äîwhich was not very much, if you remember. He invited himself down one weekend when the house was very full, and he seemed to make a point of consulting nobody‚Äôs convenience but his own. And you know, dear, you even said yourself you thought he was unnecessarily rude to poor old Lord Mountweazle.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe said what he thought,‚Äù said Mary. ‚ÄúOf course, Lord Mountweazle, poor dear, doesn‚Äôt understand that the present generation is accustomed to discuss things with its elders, not just kowtow to them. When George gave his opinion, he thought he was just contradicting.‚Äù

‚ÄúTo be sure,‚Äù said the Dowager, ‚Äúwhen you flatly deny everything a person says it does sound like contradiction to the uninitiated. But all I remember saying to Peter was that Mr.¬ÝGoyles‚Äôs manners seemed to me to lack polish, and that he showed a lack of independence in his opinions.‚Äù

‚ÄúA lack of independence?‚Äù said Mary, wide-eyed.

‚ÄúWell, dear, I thought so. What oft was thought and frequently much better expressed, as Pope saysÔªø‚Äîor was it somebody else? But the worse you express yourself these days the more profound people think youÔªø‚Äîthough that‚Äôs nothing new. Like Browning and those quaint metaphysical people, when you never know whether they really mean their mistress or the Established Church, so bridegroomy and biblicalÔªø‚Äîto say nothing of dear St.¬ÝAugustineÔªø‚Äîthe Hippo man, I mean, not the one who missionized over here, though I daresay he was delightful too, and in those days I suppose they didn‚Äôt have annual sales of work and tea in the parish room, so it doesn‚Äôt seem quite like what we mean nowadays by missionariesÔªø‚Äîhe knew all about itÔªø‚Äîyou remember about that mandrakeÔªø‚Äîor is that the thing you had to get a big black dog for? Manichee, that‚Äôs the word. What was his name? Was it Faustus? Or am I mixing him up with the old man in the opera?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, anyway,‚Äù said Mary, without stopping to disentangle the Duchess‚Äôs sequence of ideas, ‚ÄúGeorge was the only person I really cared aboutÔªø‚Äîhe still is. Only it did seem so hopeless. Perhaps you didn‚Äôt say much about him, mother, but Gerald said lotsÔªø‚Äîdreadful things!‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said the Duchess, ‚Äúhe said what he thought. The present generation does, you know. To the uninitiated, I admit, dear, it does sound a little rude.‚Äù

Peter grinned, but Mary went on unheeding.

‚ÄúGeorge had simply no money. He‚Äôd really given everything he had to the Labor Party one way and another, and he‚Äôd lost his job in the Ministry of Information: they found he had too much sympathy with the Socialists abroad. It was awfully unfair. Anyhow, one couldn‚Äôt be a burden on him; and Gerald was a beast, and said he‚Äôd absolutely stop my allowance if I didn‚Äôt send George away. So I did, but of course it didn‚Äôt make a bit of difference to the way we both felt. I will say for mother she was a bit more decent. She said she‚Äôd help us if George got a job; but, as I pointed out, if George got a job we shouldn‚Äôt need helping!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut, my dear, I could hardly insult Mr.¬ÝGoyles by suggesting that he should live on his mother-in-law,‚Äù said the Dowager.

‚ÄúWhy not?‚Äù said Mary. ‚ÄúGeorge doesn‚Äôt believe in those old-fashioned ideas about property. Besides, if you‚Äôd given it to me, it would be my money. We believe in men and women being equal. Why should the one always be the breadwinner more than the other?‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt imagine, dear,‚Äù said the Dowager. ‚ÄúStill, I could hardly expect poor Mr.¬ÝGoyles to live on unearned increment when he didn‚Äôt believe in inherited property.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs a fallacy,‚Äù said Mary, rather vaguely. ‚ÄúAnyhow,‚Äù she added hastily, ‚Äúthat‚Äôs what happened. Then, after the war, George went to Germany to study Socialism and Labor questions there, and nothing seemed any good. So when Denis Cathcart turned up, I said I‚Äôd marry him.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy?‚Äù asked Peter. ‚ÄúHe never sounded to me a bit the kind of bloke for you. I mean, as far as I could make out, he was Tory and diplomatic andÔªø‚Äîwell, quite crusted old tawny, so to speak. I shouldn‚Äôt have thought you had an idea in common.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo; but then he didn‚Äôt care twopence whether I had any ideas or not. I made him promise he wouldn‚Äôt bother me with diplomats and people, and he said no, I could do as I liked, provided I didn‚Äôt compromise him. And we were to live in Paris and go our own ways and not bother. And anything was better than staying here, and marrying somebody in one‚Äôs own set, and opening bazaars and watching polo and meeting the Prince of Wales. So I said I‚Äôd marry Denis, because I didn‚Äôt care about him, and I‚Äôm pretty sure he didn‚Äôt care a halfpenny about me, and we should have left each other alone. I did so want to be left alone!‚Äù

‚ÄúWas Jerry all right about your money?‚Äù inquired Peter.

‚ÄúOh, yes. He said Denis was no great catchÔªø‚ÄîI do wish Gerald wasn‚Äôt so vulgar, in that flat, early-Victorian wayÔªø‚Äîbut he said that, after George, he could only thank his stars it wasn‚Äôt worse.‚Äù

‚ÄúMake a note of that, Charles,‚Äù said Wimsey.

‚ÄúWell, it seemed all right at first, but, as things went on, I got more and more depressed. Do you know, there was something a little alarming about Denis. He was so extraordinarily reserved. I know I wanted to be left alone, butÔªø‚Äîwell, it was uncanny! He was correct. Even when he went off the deep end and was passionateÔªø‚Äîwhich didn‚Äôt often happenÔªø‚Äîhe was correct about it. Extraordinary. Like one of those odd French novels, you know, Peter: frightfully hot stuff, but absolutely impersonal.‚Äù

‚ÄúCharles, old man!‚Äù said Lord Peter.

‚ÄúM‚Äôm?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs important. You realize the bearing of that?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo.‚Äù

‚ÄúNever mind. Drive on, Polly.‚Äù

‚ÄúAren‚Äôt I making your head ache?‚Äù

‚ÄúDamnably; but I like it. Do go on. I‚Äôm not sprouting a lily with anguish moist and fever-dew, or anything like that. I‚Äôm getting really thrilled. What you‚Äôve just said is more illuminating than anything I‚Äôve struck for a week.‚Äù

‚ÄúReally!‚Äù Mary stared at Peter with every trace of hostility vanished. ‚ÄúI thought you‚Äôd never understand that part.‚Äù

‚ÄúLord!‚Äù said Peter. ‚ÄúWhy not?‚Äù

Mary shook her head. ‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôd been corresponding all the time with George, and suddenly he wrote to me at the beginning of this month to say he‚Äôd come back from Germany, and had got a job on the ThunderclapÔªø‚Äîthe Socialist weekly, you knowÔªø‚Äîat a beginning screw of ¬£4 a week, and wouldn‚Äôt I chuck these capitalists and so on, and come and be an honest working woman with him. He could get me a secretarial job on the paper. I was to type and so on for him, and help him get his articles together. And he thought between us we should make ¬£6 or ¬£7 a week, which would be heaps to live on. And I was getting more frightened of Denis every day. So I said I would. But I knew there‚Äôd be an awful row with Gerald. And really I was rather ashamedÔªø‚Äîthe engagement had been announced and there‚Äôd be a ghastly lot of talk and people trying to persuade me. And Denis might have made things horribly uncomfortable for GeraldÔªø‚Äîhe was rather that sort. So we decided the best thing to do would be just to run away and get married first, and escape the wrangling.‚Äù

‚ÄúQuite so,‚Äù said Peter. ‚ÄúBesides, it would look rather well in the paper, wouldn‚Äôt it? ‚ÄòPeer‚Äôs Daughter Weds SocialistÔªø‚ÄîRomantic Sidecar ElopementÔªø‚Äî‚Äú¬£6 a week Plenty,‚Äù says Her Ladyship.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúPig!‚Äù said Lady Mary.

‚ÄúVery good,‚Äù said Peter, ‚ÄúI get you! So it was arranged that the romantic Goyles should fetch you away from RiddlesdaleÔªø‚Äîwhy Riddlesdale? It would be twice as easy from London or Denver.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo. For one thing he had to be up North. And everybody knows one in town, andÔªø‚Äîanyhow, we didn‚Äôt want to wait.‚Äù

‚ÄúBesides, one would miss the Young Lochinvar touch. Well, then, why at the unearthly hour of 3¬Ýa.m.?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe had a meeting on Wednesday night at Northallerton. He was going to come straight on and pick me up, and run me down to town to be married by special license. We allowed ample time. George had to be at the office next day.‚Äù

‚ÄúI see. Well, I‚Äôll go on now, and you stop me if I‚Äôm wrong. You went up at 9:30 on Wednesday night. You packed a suitcase. YouÔªø‚Äîdid you think of writing any sort of letter to comfort your sorrowing friends and relations?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, I wrote one. But IÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course. Then you went to bed, I fancy, or, at any rate, turned the clothes back and lay down.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. I lay down. It was a good thing I did, as it happenedÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúTrue, you wouldn‚Äôt have had much time to make the bed look probable in the morning, and we should have heard about it. By the way, Parker, when Mary confessed her sins to you last night, did you make any notes?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Parker, ‚Äúif you can read my shorthand.‚Äù

‚ÄúQuite so,‚Äù said Peter. ‚ÄúWell, the rumpled bed disposes of your story about never having gone to bed at all, doesn‚Äôt it?‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd I thought it was such a good story!‚Äù

‚ÄúWant of practice,‚Äù replied her brother kindly. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll do better, next time. It‚Äôs just as well, really, that it‚Äôs so hard to tell a long, consistent lie. Did you, as a matter of fact, hear Gerald go out at 11:30, as Pettigrew-Robinson (damn his ears!) said?‚Äù

‚ÄúI fancy I did hear somebody moving about,‚Äù said Mary, ‚Äúbut I didn‚Äôt think much about it.‚Äù

‚ÄúQuite right,‚Äù said Peter, ‚Äúwhen I hear people movin‚Äô about the house at night, I‚Äôm much too delicate-minded to think anything at all.‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course,‚Äù interposed the Duchess, ‚Äúparticularly in England, where it is so oddly improper to think. I will say for Peter that, if he can put a continental interpretation on anything, he willÔªø‚Äîso considerate of you, dear, as soon as you took to doing it in silence and not mentioning it, as you so intelligently did as a child. You were really a very observant little boy, dear.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd still is,‚Äù said Mary, smiling at Peter with surprising friendliness.

‚ÄúOld bad habits die hard,‚Äù said Wimsey. ‚ÄúTo proceed. At three o‚Äôclock you went down to meet Goyles. Why did he come all the way up to the house? It would have been safer to meet him in the lane.‚Äù

‚ÄúI knew I couldn‚Äôt get out of the lodge-gate without waking Hardraw, and so I‚Äôd have to get over the palings somewhere. I might have managed alone, but not with a heavy suitcase. So, as George would have to climb over, anyhow, we thought he‚Äôd better come and help carry the suitcase. And then we couldn‚Äôt miss each other by the conservatory door. I sent him a little plan of the path.‚Äù

‚ÄúWas Goyles there when you got downstairs?‚Äù

‚ÄúNoÔªø‚Äîat leastÔªø‚Äîno, I didn‚Äôt see him. But there was poor Denis‚Äôs body, and Gerald bending over it. My first idea was that Gerald had killed George. That‚Äôs why I said, ‚ÄòOh, God! you‚Äôve killed him!‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù (Peter glanced across at Parker and nodded.) ‚ÄúThen Gerald turned him over, and I saw it was DenisÔªø‚Äîand then I‚Äôm sure I heard something moving a long way off in the shrubberyÔªø‚Äîa noise like twigs snappingÔªø‚Äîand it suddenly came over me, where was George? Oh, Peter, I saw everything then, so clearly. I saw that Denis must have come on George waiting there, and attacked himÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôm sure Denis must have attacked him. Probably he thought it was a burglar. Or he found out who he was and tried to drive him away. And in the struggle George must have shot him. It was awful!‚Äù

Peter patted his sister on the shoulder. ‚ÄúPoor kid,‚Äù he said.

‚ÄúI didn‚Äôt know what to do,‚Äù went on the girl. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd so awfully little time, you see. My one idea was that nobody must suspect anybody had been there. So I had quickly to invent an excuse for being there myself. I shoved my suitcase behind the cactus-plants to start with. Jerry was taken up with the body and didn‚Äôt noticeÔªø‚Äîyou know, Jerry never does notice things till you shove them under his nose. But I knew if there‚Äôd been a shot Freddy and the Marchbankses must have heard it. So I pretended I‚Äôd heard it too, and rushed down to look for burglars. It was a bit lame, but the best thing I could think of. Gerald sent me up to alarm the house, and I had the story all ready by the time I reached the landing. Oh, and I was quite proud of myself for not forgetting the suitcase!‚Äù

‚ÄúYou dumped it into the chest,‚Äù said Peter.

‚ÄúYes. I had a horrible shock the other morning when I found you looking in.‚Äù

‚ÄúNothing like the shock I had when I found the silver sand there.‚Äù

‚ÄúSilver sand?‚Äù

‚ÄúOut of the conservatory.‚Äù

‚ÄúGood gracious!‚Äù said Mary.

‚ÄúWell, go on. You knocked up Freddy and the Pettigrew-Robinsons. Then you had to bolt into your room to destroy your farewell letter and take your clothes off.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. I‚Äôm afraid I didn‚Äôt do that very naturally. But I couldn‚Äôt expect anybody to believe that I went burglar-hunting in a complete set of silk undies and a carefully knotted tie with a gold safety-pin.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo. I see your difficulty.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt turned out quite well, too, because they were all quite ready to believe that I wanted to escape from Mrs.¬ÝPettigrew-RobinsonÔªø‚Äîexcept Mrs.¬ÝP. herself, of course.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; even Parker swallowed that, didn‚Äôt you, old man?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, quite, quite so,‚Äù said Parker gloomily.

‚ÄúI made a dreadful mistake about that shot,‚Äù resumed Lady Mary. ‚ÄúYou see, I explained it all so elaboratelyÔªø‚Äîand then I found that nobody had heard a shot at all. And afterwards they discovered that it had all happened in the shrubberyÔªø‚Äîand the time wasn‚Äôt right, either. Then at the inquest I had to stick to my storyÔªø‚Äîand it got to look worse and worseÔªø‚Äîand then they put the blame on Gerald. In my wildest moments I‚Äôd never thought of that. Of course, I see now how my wretched evidence helped.‚Äù

‚ÄúHence the ipecacuanha,‚Äù said Peter.

‚ÄúI‚Äôd got into such a frightful tangle,‚Äù said poor Lady Mary, ‚ÄúI thought I had better shut up altogether for fear of making things still worse.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd did you still think Goyles had done it?‚Äù

‚ÄúIÔªø‚ÄîI didn‚Äôt know what to think,‚Äù said the girl. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know. Peter, who else could have done it?‚Äù

‚ÄúHonestly, old thing,‚Äù said his lordship, ‚Äúif he didn‚Äôt do it, I don‚Äôt know who did.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe ran away, you see,‚Äù said Lady Mary.

‚ÄúHe seems rather good at shootin‚Äô and runnin‚Äô away,‚Äù said Peter grimly.

‚ÄúIf he hadn‚Äôt done that to you,‚Äù said Mary slowly, ‚ÄúI‚Äôd never have told you. I‚Äôd have died first. But of course, with his revolutionary doctrinesÔªø‚Äîand when you think of red Russia and all the blood spilt in riots and insurrections and thingsÔªø‚ÄîI suppose it does teach a contempt for human life.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear,‚Äù said the Duchess, ‚Äúit seems to me that Mr.¬ÝGoyles shows no especial contempt for his own life. You must try to look at the thing fairly. Shooting people and running away is not very heroicÔªø‚Äîaccording to our standards.‚Äù

‚ÄúThe thing I don‚Äôt understand,‚Äù struck in Wimsey hurriedly, ‚Äúis how Gerald‚Äôs revolver got into the shrubbery.‚Äù

‚ÄúThe thing I should like to know about,‚Äù said the Duchess, ‚Äúis, was Denis really a cardsharper?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe thing I should like to know about,‚Äù said Parker, ‚Äúis the green-eyed cat.‚Äù

‚ÄúDenis never gave me a cat,‚Äù said Mary. ‚ÄúThat was a tarradiddle.‚Äù

‚ÄúWere you ever in a jeweler‚Äôs with him in the Rue de la Paix?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, yes; heaps of times. And he gave me a diamond and tortoiseshell comb. But never a cat.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen we may disregard the whole of last night‚Äôs elaborate confession,‚Äù said Lord Peter, looking through Parker‚Äôs notes, with a smile. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs really not bad, Polly, not bad at all. You‚Äôve quite a talent for romantic fictionÔªø‚Äîno, I mean it! Just here and there you need more attention to detail. For instance, you couldn‚Äôt have dragged that badly wounded man all up the path to the house without getting blood all over your coat, you know. By the way, did Goyles know Cathcart at all?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot to my knowledge.‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause Parker and I had an alternative theory, which would clear Goyles from the worst part of the charge, anyhow. Tell her, old man; it was your idea.‚Äù

Thus urged, Parker outlined the blackmail and suicide theory.

‚ÄúThat sounds plausible,‚Äù said MaryÔªø‚Äî‚Äúacademically speaking, I mean; but it isn‚Äôt a bit like GeorgeÔªø‚ÄîI mean, blackmail is so beastly, isn‚Äôt it?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell,‚Äù said Peter, ‚ÄúI think the best thing is to go and see Goyles. Whatever the key to Wednesday night‚Äôs riddle is, he holds it. Parker, old man, we‚Äôre nearing the end of the chase.‚Äù
